name: OCaml 5 — Multicore Domain GC
summary: OCaml 5.0 (December 2022) shipped a stop-the-world parallel minor copying collector + mostly-concurrent mark-sweep major collector, with one private minor heap per domain and a shared major heap.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
OCaml 5.0 (December 2022) shipped a stop-the-world parallel minor copying collector + mostly-concurrent mark-sweep major collector, with one private minor heap per *domain* and a shared major heap.

The cleanest example of retrofitting multicore onto a previously single-threaded GC.

sections.title: Per-domain minor heap and parallel collection
sections.content:
**STW parallel minor collection**: all domains promote in parallel; conflicting promotions of the same object are serialized via interrupts. Each domain has its own minor heap (fast bump allocation; copying collection on minor-heap full).

The "parallel" property is per-collection: when a minor GC fires, all domains stop, but each domain processes its own minor heap in parallel with the others. No domain blocks on another domain's GC work.

sections.title: Shared major heap with idempotent marking
sections.content:
**Idempotent marking + disjoint sweeping** on the shared major heap — domains can mark the same object redundantly and each sweeps only its own pool slice; tiny STW only at major-cycle end.

The idempotent-marking design is the key insight: instead of synchronising domains during marking (which would force expensive coordination), let each domain mark whatever it sees, accepting redundant work in exchange for lock-free progress. Disjoint sweeping is partition-by-domain so no two domains touch the same major-heap pool slice during reclamation.

sections.title: Dirty-stack tracking for fibers
sections.content: **Dirty-stack tracking** for fibers: stacks not currently running may temporarily violate the strong tricolor invariant, cleaned at minor GCs. This is necessary because OCaml 5 fibers are first-class continuations whose stacks can be paused, resumed, and migrated between domains; the GC marks must converge regardless of which domain runs the fiber next.

sections.title: Compatibility and design lesson
sections.content:
Backwards-compatible with single-domain code and the C API. The **cleanest example of retrofitting multicore onto a previously single-threaded GC**.

Lessons for new language designers: a minor-heap-per-domain + shared-major-heap structure is a workable middle point between Erlang's strict process isolation (BEAM) and the JVM's fully shared heap. Idempotent marking is the technique that makes mostly-concurrent multicore GC tractable without per-edge synchronisation.
